coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation strategy of novel B2O3-modified carbon dots with 1.99 s ultra-long Room-Temperature phosphorescence for multidimensional encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Xilang
Zhao, Huaqi
Bai, Haiyan
Ding, Liu
Chen, Weixing
coredata.dc:description: Facile synthesis of Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) with super stability and long-afterglow are of great significance, but hard to achieve. Herein, a brilliant gram-scale and solvent-free pyrolysis treatment strategy has been developed to prepare high-performance URTP carbon dots (CDs) by regulating different temperature (250–500 °C). The optimized CDs (CD-400) showed room-temperature phosphorescence 1.99 s and lasting over 22 s to naked eyes, which is superior to most of the reported URTP CDs. Owing to the stabilization effects of the modified B2O3 layer on the surface, the homogenous distribution of CD-400 with the narrow diameter of 1.44 nm was constructed, displaying a superb stability through hydrogen-bond network. In addition, the doping atoms (N, O) greatly enhanced the n-π* transitions and stabilized triplet excitons radiative transitions, facilitating the effective intersystem crossing (ISC) and the RTP emissions. More importantly, the B2O3-modified CDs were successfully applied in the multi-level information encryption (time-resolved RTP performance) and fingerprint identification (bifurcation, whorl and termination details).
coredata.dcterms:subject: URTP
B2O3-modified CDs
Solvent-free method
Anti-counterfeiting
Fingerprint identification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of B2O3-modified CDs
The mechanism of URTP for B2O3-modified CD-400

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of URTP CDs. (b) the photographs of the URTP CDs powders under 365 UV lamp for CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 and CD-500.

(a-c) the particle size distribution maps of CD-250, CD-300 and CD-400. (e-f) TEM images of CD-250, CD-300 and CD-400. (g) XRD of the CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 and CD-500. (h) Raman spectra of the CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 and CD-500. (i) FI-IR spectra of the BA, Urea and B2O3-modified CD-400.

(a) HR-XPS of B2O3-modified CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 and CD-500. C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c), B 1 s (d) and O 1 s (e) spectra of the B2O3-modified CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 and CD-500. (f) TGA of the CD-400 powders.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders. (d) RTP emission spectra of the B2O3-modified CD-400 powders at different excitation wavelengths under ambient conditions. (e) RTP decay spectra of the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder. (f) Phosphorescence emission spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 powder recorded at different temperatures with the excitation at 400 nm.

(a) Scheme illustration of the possible chemical composition of B2O3-modified CD-400 powder. (b) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders at low temperature (77 K). (c) The fluorescence / phosphorescence emission process and mechanism of B2O3-modified CD-400 powder.

(a) Double-modal switching application of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders. (b) Data encryption application of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders. (c) Data encryption application of B2O3-modified CD-250, CD-300 and CD-400 powders.

(a) The digital encryption of the “XATU” and the observed patterns with daylight, UV light on and off, respectively. (b) Images of B2O3-modified CD-400 powders-stained fingerprints on the slide with daylight, UV light on and off, respectively.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation strategy of novel B2O3-modified carbon dots with 1.99 s ultra-long Room-Temperature phosphorescence for multidimensional encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials, Universities of Shaanxi Province, School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials
School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials, Universities of Shaanxi Province, School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yulin Boyi-Jingking Research Institute of Industrial Technology Development Research, Yulin, Shaanxi Province 719054, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yulin Boyi-Jingking Research Institute of Industrial Technology Development Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yulin Boyi-Jingking Research Institute of Industrial Technology Development Research, Yulin, Shaanxi Province 719054, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Technological University, Xi'an 710032, Shaanxi, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A brilliant gram-scale and solvent-free pyrolysis treatment strategy has been developed to fabricate high-performance URTP carbon dots (CDs) by regulating different temperature (250–500 °C). The optimized URTP CDs (CD-400) showed ultra-long 1.99 s and lasting over 22 s to naked eyes, superioring to most of the reported URTP CDs.

Facile synthesis of Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) with super stability and long-afterglow are of great significance, but hard to achieve. Herein, a brilliant gram-scale and solvent-free pyrolysis treatment strategy has been developed to prepare high-performance URTP carbon dots (CDs) by regulating different temperature (250–500 °C). The optimized CDs (CD-400) showed room-temperature phosphorescence 1.99 s and lasting over 22 s to naked eyes, which is superior to most of the reported URTP CDs. Owing to the stabilization effects of the modified B2O3 layer on the surface, the homogenous distribution of CD-400 with the narrow diameter of 1.44 nm was constructed, displaying a superb stability through hydrogen-bond network. In addition, the doping atoms (N, O) greatly enhanced the n-π* transitions and stabilized triplet excitons radiative transitions, facilitating the effective intersystem crossing (ISC) and the RTP emissions. More importantly, the B2O3-modified CDs were successfully applied in the multi-level information encryption (time-resolved RTP performance) and fingerprint identification (bifurcation, whorl and termination details).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have been widely used in areas of information encryption, molecular switches, optoelectronic devices and bioimaging, especially the Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) materials with the ultra-long afterglow emission caught by naked eyes [1–5]. Until now, many types of RTP materials have been reported, such as metal-doped inorganic compounds, small molecular organic compounds and carbon dots (CDs) with various chemical and physical properties [6–9]. Among them, RTP CDs materials have shown great superiorities owing to abundant surface functional groups, excellent optical performance and cost-effective preparation [10–13]. However, most RTP CDs suffer from relatively short lifetime (hundreds of milliseconds) and delay time (only last a few seconds being visible to the naked eyes) [14,15]. The short delay time may be caused by inefficient intersystem crossing (ISC) and intensive nonradiative decay, which greatly hinder their further applications [16–18]. Moreover, the variation of the nanostructure has great influences on the performance of RTP. At present, there is no effective method to precisely regulate nanostructure of CDs to improve its RTP performance. Therefore, exploring the facile synthesis and structural control methods of the URTP CDs with the long afterglow emission is of great significance.

Some effective strategies have been explored to achieve high RTP performances [19–23]. On the one hand, embedding CDs into various matrix materials (e.g. inorganic salts, zeolites, and polyvinyl alcohol) to effectively suppress the non-radiative transitions by constructing a rigid environment [24,25]. However, the synthesis of these composites generally involve multiple preparation steps and homogenous distribution of CDs in the matrix is difficult [26–28]. On the other hand, some one-pot synthetic methods have also been reported for achieving high-performance surficial modification of CDs [29,30]. Doping heteroatoms (e.g., N, P, Br, I) or functional group (e.g., C = O, C = N) in CDs by designing the synthetic precursors which favor n-π* transitions into CDs to promote the effective ISC process of the hybrids [31–33]. The heteroatoms or hetero-interfaces on the surface of the CDs composite play crucial roles for obtaining high RTP behavior.

Herein, we report a gram-scale and solvent-free synthetic strategy for the construction of ultra-stable uniform B2O3-modified CDs with URTP effects under the pyrolysis of 400 °C. The as-synthesized B2O3-modified CD-400 presented a long RTP lifetime to be 1.99 s and lasted over 22 s to naked eyes, which is superior to most of the reported CDs. The doped heteroatoms (N, O) and rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network greatly enhanced the n-π* transitions and stabilized triplet excitons radiative transitions, promoting the effective intersystem crossing (ISC) and the RTP behavior. Moreover, the B2O3-modified CDs could be promising commercial materials in multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption owing to their distinctive URTP performance. This work provides an excellent example for the large-scale synthesizing of CDs-based URTP materials and preventing aggregation-caused quenching of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of B2O3-modified CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The large-scale and solvent-free synthetic route of B2O3-modified CDs was illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The urea and excess boric acid(H3BO3) powders were mixed and heated by the solvent-free carbonization method at different temperature (250 °C, 300 °C, 400 °C, 500 °C) for 5 h, transforming into CD-250, CD-300, CD-400, CD-500, respectively. The as-prepared four B2O3-modified CDs all exhibited colorless under daylight and showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV-365 light irradiation. Interestingly, after ceasing the UV light irradiation, the four B2O3-modified CDs powders showed intense green phosphorescence under room temperature conditions with different lasting time between 6 and 22 s (Fig. 1b and Video S1). Among them, the superior 22 s delay time for CD-400 was observed, which far beyond most of the reported URTP CDs. The excess H3BO3 in the precursors provided the surficial B2O3 layer on the final CDs products which directly determined the URTP behavior.

The morphology and chemical structures of B2O3-modified CDs were characterized for investigating the luminescent properties and mechanism. Firstly, the morphologies were characterized through transmission electron microscope (TEM), displaying the uniformly dispersion of the CD-250, CD-300, CD-400 particles with the average sizes of ∼ 1.40, 1.46, 1.44 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a-c). All these CDs materials showed homogenously practical distribution with uniform size, which was benefit for the luminance properties. In HR-TEM image (2d-e), the distinct lattice spacing of ∼ 0.20 nm was assigned to the (100) plane of h-BN [34].

Furthermore, the chemical structures of B2O3-modified CDs were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectrum, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The XRD patterns (Fig. 2g) exhibited that the sharp diffraction peak at 28° was gradually distinct by the increment of the temperature (250–500 °C), which could be assigned to B2O3. The results demonstrated that the formation of polycrystalline B2O3 matrix in the CDs during the heating process [35]. The typical peak at 22.6° gradually become clearer from CD-250 to CD-500, indicating the amorphous glassy state in CD-400 and CD-500 was improved gradually. The weak peak at 42° should correspond to be (100) face of h-BN [36]. In Raman spectrum, a characteristic peak at 1399 cm−1 was assigned to the h-BN structure, which was consistent with XRD results (Fig. 2h).

As exhibited in Fig. 2i, the absorption peak at 3232 cm−1 and 1712 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H and C = O groups, which demonstrated the effective surface functionalization of B2O3-modified CD-400 [37]. The characteristic absorption peaks of B-O and B-O–H were at 1439 cm−1 and 1199 cm−1. The peaks located at around 1372 cm−1 and 725 cm−1 correspond to in-plane B-N-B stretching and out-of-plane B-N bending vibrations, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1085 cm−1 and 925 cm−1 were ascribed to C-O-B and B-C interactions, indicating effective doping of O, N atoms in B2O3-modified CD-400 [38,39]. The results revealed that the abundant –OH/–NH2 groups located on the surface of O, N-doped CD-400, which not only facilitated hydrogen-bonded formation on the interface of CDs and B2O3 matrix, but also prevented the aggregation of the B2O3-modified CDs.

The full XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a) of B2O3-modified CD-400 further revealed four typical peaks: C 1 s (297.98 eV), N 1 s (409.98 eV), B 1 s (199.98 eV), O 1 s (544.98 eV). As exhibited in Fig. 3b-3e, the high resolution XPS spectrum of B 1 s exhibited three binding energies at 192.75 eV, 193.58 eV, 194.60 eV that could be assigned to BCO2, B2O3, B-O, demonstrating the formation of B2O3 matrix via dehydration reaction from excess H3BO3 [40]. The high resolution C 1 s spectra indicated the presence of C-N (284.70 eV), C-O (286.20 eV) and C = O (288.55 eV). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum was fitted to three peaks of R-NH2 (400.34 eV), N–H (401.96 eV) and C-N (402.96 eV). Similarly, the presence of C = O, C-O-B/C-O-C and O-C = O/C-OH could be distinguished in the high resolution O 1 s due to the observation of three peaks at 532.67 eV, 533.13 eV and 534.29 eV, respectively [41]. The XPS results confirmed that the N,O-doped CDs as luminescence centers generated and tightly embedding in the polycrystalline B2O3 matrix.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) results showed the B2O3-modified CD-400 was favorable thermo-stability, which may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen-bond skeleton between CD-400 and B2O3 (Fig. 3f). The mass loss of −10% was observed upon heating from 150 to 180 °C, owing to the evaporation of surface adsorbent water and crystal water. Furthermore, the phosphorescence property could maintain after six months storage under ambient conditions (Figure S2-S5), demonstrating the high stability of the URTP CDs.

Based on the above results, the modified B2O3 layer on the surface not only greatly enhanced hydrogen bonding to stabilize the CDs, but also efficiently promote triplet excitons to suppress inactivation from molecular collision in the ambient environment.

The UV–Vis spectrum of solid-state CD-400 displayed two broad absorption bands centered at 296 and 347 nm (Fig. 4 a), which should be attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C and n-π* transition of C = O [42]. In Fig. 4b, the B2O3-modified CD-400 exhibited excitation-dependent emission feature, illustrating that the existence of multiple emissive centers. Along with the increment of the excitation wavelength from 260 to 360 nm, the emission wavelength gradually exhibited a slightly redshift. The optimum excitation wavelength centered at 300 nm (Fig. 4c) was in good accordance with the absorption wavelength (296 nm), indicating that the fluorescence emission could be resulted from the surface C = O/C = C relevant moieties.

In the phosphorescence spectrum (Fig. 4d), the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder displayed the maximum emission with a slightly redshift when the excitation wavelength increasing from 280 to 360 nm, indicating that the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder with the excitation wavelength-dependence of RTP behavior. The photoluminescent lifetime of the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder was further confirmed by the transient photoluminescent decay spectrum (Fig. 4e). The lifetime of two components was fitted by a biexponential function, giving a result of 0.33 s (13.99 %) and 2.26 s (86.01 %), respectively. Furthermore, the average lifetime (
τ
a v g
) of the B2O3-modified CD-400 was calculated to be 1.99 s with the excitation of 300 nm based on the following equation (1):
(1)
τ
avg
= ∑
α i
τ i 2
/ ∑
α i
τ i

The temperature-dependent transient photoluminescent decays exhibited that the phosphorescence emission intensity (Fig. 4f) of the B2O3-modified CD-400 powders declined gradually with the temperature increasing from 77 K to 298 K. It was attribute to the cryogenic temperature suppressed the intermolecular motions and reduced the non-radiative relaxation, which was well coincided with the feature of RTP phenomenon. The absolute fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yield of the B2O3-modified CD-400 were about 22.15 % and 15.15 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of URTP for B2O3-modified CD-400
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism for URTP origin of B2O3-modified CD-400 was evaluated (Fig. 5 a). First of all, the doped heteroatoms (N, O) could facilitated the spin–orbit coupling and reduced the energy gap of the singlet-to-triplet states, promoting the efficiency of intersystem crossing [39]. In this work, the raw materials of H3BO3 and urea served as carbon source for CDs and provided N, O doping atoms. After high-temperature dehydration, condensation and carbonization, the precursors formed specific sub-fluorophores functional groups (C = O / C = N) responsible for luminescence, including the dense core and a lot of external groups (–OH and –NH2). To further confirm the RTP origin of the B2O3-modified CD-400, the H3BO3 or urea alone was selected as precursors at the same experimental conditions. The as-prepared product showed obviously weaker fluorescence emissions and RTP performances (0.70 s and 0.86 s, Figure S10) than those of B2O3-modified CD-400, suggesting the specific sub-fluorophores promoted the RTP effect process.

Furthermore, H3BO3 was used to react with urea to form the carbon dots and further dehydrated to form boron oxide with the increment of the temperature. Moreover, the –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of CD-400 were conducive to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CD-400 and B2O3, and benefit to the homogenous dispersion of CD-400. For further understand the structure of B2O3-modified CD-400, the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder was conducted via dialysis treatment. The obtained CD-400-dialysis only emit strong blue fluorescence with faint RTP performance (0.69 s) (Figure S11a), indicating that the modified B2O3 layer greatly promote the RTP effects. Furthermore, the ratio of urea and H3BO3 (1:1) was changed, which exhibited weaker RTP (1.53 s) (Figure S11b), suggesting that the lack of B2O3 layer led to the difficulty of forming the network structure. Therefore, it could be speculated that the strong RTP was emerged by of B2O3-modified CD-400 was fixed by the rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network [34].

Moreover, the energy gaps (△EST ) between lowest singlet state (S1) and triplet state (T1) could be quantitatively evaluated by fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of B2O3-modified CD-400 at low temperature (77 K). Accordingly, the S1, T1 and △EST values were calculated as 2.51 eV, 2.42 eV and 0.09 eV, respectively, which was favorable for the effective ISC process to produce triplet excitons (Fig. 5b-c).

Owing to the extraordinary RTP property of B2O3-modified CD-400, the anti-counterfeiting and information encryption properties of B2O3-modified CD-400 based materials have been constructed and evaluated. Fig. 6 a showed a simple dual-modal switching encryption by loading B2O3-modified CD-400 powders into “tiger” mould. The tiger pattern was naked white in sunlight and displayed bright blue fluorescence under the UV 365 nm irradiation. Once removing the excitation light, the luminous green phosphorescence of the tiger pattern was observed. The phosphorescence performance of B2O3-modified CD-400 could be quenched by water, hence it was further used in anti-counterfeiting. As exhibited in Fig. 6b, the B2O3-modified CD-400 powder was used to spell out the letters “2022”, then deionized water was sprayed to the second letter “0” and the fourth letter “2”. The “2022” pattern could be observed clearly in the sunlight. Upon UV 365 nm irradiation, the first letter “2” and the third letter “2” exhibited lightful blue fluorescence. The second letter “0” and the fourth letter “2” glowed faint blue fluorescence which was attribute to the destruction of the rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network. Once switching off the UV irradiation, only the first letter “2” and the third letter “2” exhibited bright green phosphorescence.

Furthermore, due to the different RTP performance of B2O3-modified CD-400, CD-300 and CD-250, the time-resolved RTP anti-counterfeiting was carried out. As shown in Fig. 6c, the graphic pattern “flower” was designed with the petals, branches and leaves were drawn by B2O3-modified CD-400, CD-300 and CD-250, respectively. The bright blue “flower” was observed under the excitation of UV 365 lamp. After the removal of the excitation light, the clearly bright-green “flower” showed up. Because of the remarkable difference in afterglow time among these RTP CDs, the phosphorescence pattern could last for several seconds and the leaves (B2O3-modified CD-250) of the bright-green “flower” gradually disappeared. Then only the phosphorescence of petals (CD-400) lasted for 12 s.

Inspired by the time-resolved RTP performance, the microarray data storage pattern (Fig. 7 a) was fabricated according to the binary codes of American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII characters) [43,44]. The bright and dark pore represented number “1″ and “0”. Under UV illumination, every pore exhibited blue fluorescence signals. After turning off the UV lamp, multi-level encryption codes were displayed in turn. The real information could be easily read out due to the time-resolved RTP performance, and the signals translated into “01111000”, “01100001”, “0110100” and “01110101”, corresponding to letter “x”, “a”, “t” and “u” which was the abbreviation of Xi’an Technology University. The results provided efficient methods to extend the RTP materials in the field of time-dependent anti-counterfeiting. Moreover, fingerprints had become one of the important ID card for human beings [29]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, [45] RTP materials were suitable for fingerprint recognition which could avoid the environmental background luminescence. Herein, the finger firstly pressed on the aluminum foil, the B2O3-modified CD-400 powders were evenly spread on the latent fingerprint and held for several seconds. Then the phosphorescent fingerprint was obtained after removing excess powders. Under the irradiation of 365 nm, the outline of the fingerprint could be observed (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the contrast ratio was relatively low due to the interference of auto-fluorescence background. After ceasing the UV 365 nm irradiation, the high-quality and holonomic green fingerprint was clearly identified. Moreover, the fine fingerprint details (bifurcation, whorl and termination) were clearly revealed, which was advantageous in forensic identification. The above results suggested that the developed B2O3-modified CDs could offer advanced platforms for multi-level information encryption and fingerprint identification.

In summary, a solvent-free synthesis strategy was proposed for the design and construction of B2O3-modified RTP carbon dots. The developed B2O3-modified CD-400 exhibit a long RTP lifetime of 1.99 s and the RTP phenomenon in the naked eyes for 22 s after ceasing the UV irradiation, which superior to most of the reported URTP CDs. The strong RTP was emerged by the of B2O3-modified CD-400 was fixed by the rigid B2O3 polycrystalline network. The applications of B2O3-modified CD-400 in multi-level anti-counterfeiting, digital encryption and fingerprint recognition displayed the brilliant structural and luminescent properties. More importantly, the solvent-free synthesis strategy afforded a new pathway for further constructing high-efficiency RTP materials with ultralong lifetimes.

Xilang Jin: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Huaqi Zhao: Methodology, Investigation. Haiyan Bai: Data curation, Formal analysis. Liu Ding: Validation, Methodology. Weixing Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21807085), the Natural Science Foundation Research Project of Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-087), the Technology Innovation Leading Program of Shaanxi (Program No. 2020QFY07-05) and the Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi (Program No. 2022KJXX-88).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123473.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White luminescent single-crystalline chlorinated graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Advances on Host-Guest Material Systems toward Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal Synthesis of Zinc-Doped Silica Nanospheres Simultaneously Featuring Stable Fluorescence and Long-Lived Room-Temperature Phosphorescence
